Rancho Deep Cliff HOA · April 2026 · For all homeowners

A new architectural restriction could permanently close the door on adding a 3rd bedroom for many homes in our community.

You should have recently received a notice from Community First Property Management about a proposed change to our architectural guidelines. Please read on to understand what is changing and what it means for your home.

What is being proposed

A new architectural restriction has been added to the HOA's Non-Standard Architectural Modification guidelines under "Street Facing Window Alterations." It reads:

"Replaced windows must fit in the same wall cut-outs as the existing window and the windowpane configuration cannot be altered. The wall cut-out size cannot be altered, and the cut-out cannot be relocated on the wall."

In plain terms: no street-facing window can ever be enlarged, not now and not in the future, regardless of how carefully the work is designed or how well it matches the surrounding homes.

Why window size is not just about aesthetics
5.7
sq ft minimum
clear opening
24"
minimum
height
20"
minimum
width
44"
max sill height
from floor
California Residential Code Section R319 requires every legally recognized bedroom to have an emergency egress window meeting the dimensions above. In several RDC floor plans, the only exterior wall available for a bedroom conversion contains a small bathroom window that does not meet these requirements. Enlarging it is the only path forward, and this restriction closes that path permanently.
This affects every homeowner, not just those planning renovations

Families who need more bedrooms cannot adapt their home. In today's market, moving to a larger home in Cupertino is simply not an option for most

No space for a caregiver, a returning adult child, or grandchildren staying for extended visits

Buyers with growing families will see this restriction and choose a community that gives them room to grow, narrowing our buyer pool

Resale value and long-term property appeal permanently capped for every homeowner, regardless of whether they ever plan to renovate

What the modification actually looks like

A neighbor submitted a thoughtful, professionally designed proposal for a 3-bedroom conversion. These are their actual renderings. The window change is subtle and the home reads as fully cohesive with the rest of the neighborhood. Thoughtful landscaping can soften any visual difference even further.

Front elevation — proposed 3-bed modification
3D view — same materials, roof, and color palette
Same stucco finish and color Consistent trim and framing No structural changes Subtle size difference only
The justification given
  • "It has never been done before since the builder built them"
  • "Duet homes should be mirror images"

The board has already approved window pane changes and color differences that make duet homes visually asymmetrical. Saying something has not been done before is a description of history, not a reason to restrict the future. Our CC&Rs require that architectural standards be reasonable and objective, not arbitrary (Article VIII, Section 8.01).

Our community already has variation
  • Different window colors across units
  • Different window configurations
  • Different front door styles and treatments
  • Different garage configurations

These differences exist today and the neighborhood still looks cohesive and harmonious. Variation and visual consistency have always coexisted here, and there is no reason a carefully reviewed window modification would change that.

See for yourself

These photos were taken while walking around our own community. As you can see, the variation already present across homes is real and visible — yet the neighborhood remains as beautiful and harmonious as ever. Differences in style do not mean discord.

Non-matching front windows on a duet
Different front door styles and colors
Different garage front styles
Extra side windows and doors

Our community has always balanced consistency with good judgment. A thoughtful, case-by-case review process would serve everyone far better than a permanent prohibition. We are not asking for unrestricted changes. We are asking that the door not be permanently shut on a reasonable, well-designed modification that some homeowners may genuinely need.

01
No blanket ban needed

The HOA application already states requests are reviewed case-by-case. Windows should be no different.

02
Aesthetics can be protected

Require matching frame color, trim, and materials. Review each application on its merits.

03
Precedent is not policy

Our community has grown and evolved thoughtfully over the years. Decisions that shape its future deserve the same thoughtfulness.

Take action

Email the property manager. It takes two minutes.

Send the message below to ask the board to remove this architectural restriction. Every response matters and the more homeowners who speak up, the harder this is to ignore.

Send to
cs@communityfirstpm.com
Subject: RDC - Feedback on window restriction in architectural application form
Deadline: April 30, 2026
I recently learned about the proposed architectural restriction preventing enlargement of street-facing windows. I am concerned this would effectively prevent homeowners from ever adding a 3rd bedroom in several floor plans. Due to city egress requirements, the only viable location for a bedroom window is street-facing and any other placement would result in an impractical floor plan. This limits future flexibility and potentially impacts property values for all of us. I fully support maintaining the architectural consistency of our neighborhood. However, I believe this can be achieved without a blanket restriction. Changes like these can be reviewed on a case-by-case basis through the existing architectural review process to ensure they remain cohesive and appropriate. A design-review approach would be a far better balance than a permanent, blanket architectural restriction. Please reconsider or remove this proposed change.
